risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "After stratification, randomization was performed using custom Matlab® software, with a block size of n = 6 and 1:1:1 allocation ratio." Comment: This describes a systematic random process using software-based randomization with blocked allocation after stratification, which is sufficient to indicate a truly random allocation sequence.

Quote: "Participants received group allocation information in an opaque envelope. This process was completed by an investigator who was not involved in data collection." Comment: The paper explicitly reports an opaque envelope used to deliver group allocation information and indicates the allocator was separate from data collection. Although it does not state all SNOSE details verbatim, the described opaque envelope mechanism clearly indicates recruiters could not foresee the upcoming assignment before enrolment.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "This study was a single-blind, three-arm, parallel, randomized controlled trial." Comment: The trial was only single-blind, and the intervention arms were clearly distinguishable (yoga exercise, traditional exercise, and no exercise/meditation control). Participants and exercise instructors were only blinded to the study hypothesis, not to assignment, so participants and personnel delivering the intervention likely knew group allocation. Because the main outcomes included self-reported pain and physical function, knowledge of allocation could plausibly influence these outcomes, making performance bias high.

Quote: "All outcomes were measured before and immediately after the 12-week intervention and led by the same blinded assessor." Comment: The study explicitly states that a blinded assessor performed outcome measurement, so outcome assessment was blinded. The main outcomes included self-reported pain/function questionnaires and performance tests; because the assessor measuring outcomes was blinded, and the objective mobility/strength measures are unlikely to be influenced by knowledge of allocation, detection bias for this domain is low.

Quote: "One participant in TE completed only two training sessions and was lost to follow-up; therefore data from 10 participants in this group were available for per-protocol analysis." Comment: Attrition was minimal (1/31 randomized participants) and appears balanced enough not to threaten the results. The paper also states that the participant was lost to follow-up after only two sessions, while another TE participant who was unable to complete the intervention still had follow-up data and was included in analysis, suggesting most participants completed outcome assessment and there was no signal of outcome-related differential dropout across groups.
